data.title: Alpinum Investment Management Q2 2025 Letter
data.description:
“The Empire strikes back!”

In the US, President Donald Trump's January inauguration marked the start of a term with assertive trade policies. The administration implemented significant tariff hikes, including a 25% duty on Canadian steel and aluminium, a 25% tariff on European cars and a 30% levy on Chinese electronics. These moves intensified trade tensions, contributing to a 2.3% decline in the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX) and raising (short-term) recession concerns. The Federal Reserve kept interest rates at 4.5%, balancing inflation control with signs of economic slowdown.

Chart 1: No recession in sight (so far)

Europe experienced a transformative geopolitical and economic shift, led by Germany’s landmark multi-hundred billion investment program, ending austerity. The euro surged to five-month highs, further supported by Ukraine’s acceptance of a proposed ceasefire, bolstering investor confidence. In China, authorities injected USD 200 billion into the financial sector to stabilize the property market and mitigate systemic risks. Despite these efforts, investor sentiment remained fragile, with the People’s Bank of China signalling further easing measures to support growth. The quarter underscored the fragility of the global economy, as trade disputes, policy uncertainties, and financial instability continue to weigh on growth prospects.

United States

In Q1 2025, the US economy exhibited notable resilience, buoyed by a robust labour market, with GDP growth maintaining a steady pace at 2.8% for 2024, marking the third consecutive year of expansion within the 2.5-3.0% range. Despite early signs of softening, the labour market remained a pivotal driver of consumption and crucial to economic growth. However, macroeconomic factors, including heightened policy uncertainty and escalating tariff risks, presented new challenges, particularly within manufacturing and trade sectors. The Trump administration’s imposition of a 25% tariff on steel and aluminium imports, with a potential 50% escalation for Canada, later deferred following negotiations, further strained trade relations. While tariffs on Canadian and Mexican goods were temporarily paused, the administration increased tariffs on Chinese imports to 20%, triggering retaliatory actions. The European Union also faced potential tariffs, signalling the intensification of trade conflicts.

Chart 2: S&P 500 companies citing “tariffs” on earnings calls

Concurrently, the S&P 500 exhibited considerable volatility, driven by the surge in DeepSeek's valuation in January, which spurred a recalibration of AI and tech stocks. Simultaneously, the aggressive tariff policy, combined with rising trade tensions, amplified market uncertainty. This led to a 2.3% decline in the index in the first quarter of the year as investors reassessed growth prospects amid escalating geopolitical risks and inflationary pressures. The US inflation remained persistent, with the CPI decelerating to 2.8% in February from 3.0% in January. In response to persistent inflation, particularly in non-housing services, the Federal Reserve adopted a cautious stance, signalling a“higher for longer” monetary policy, with two rate cuts anticipated in 2025, while maintaining the long-term policy rate in the mid-4% range.

Europe

The European economy expanded at a subdued pace, navigating a complex landscape of monetary stimulus, fiscal interventions and intensifying geopolitical tensions. The European Central Bank (ECB) executed a 25-basis-point reduction in its benchmark rate to 2.5%, marking the sixth consecutive cut within a year. This sustained monetary accommodation sought to counteract lacklustre economic activity, prompting the ECB to revise its 2025 GDP growth forecast downwards to 0.9% from 1.1%, while inflation was projected to average 2.3%. The labour market remained resilient, though wage growth moderated amid persistent economic uncertainties. Fiscal policy diverged across the region, with Germany unveiling a landmark multi-hundred billion investment program aimed at revitalizing its domestic economy, signaling a shift away from austerity, while potentially exerting broader inflationary and growth implications across the eurozone.

Chart 3: European equities outperforming in 2025

The political landscape was reshaped by the German federal election on 23 February, where the CDU/CSU secured 28.5% of the vote, emerging as the largest parliamentary bloc. The far-right AfD achieved an unprecedented 20.8%, underscoring a shift in voter sentiment and complicating coalition negotiations, with ramifications for both domestic and EU policy trajectories. Geopolitical tensions escalated as the United States imposed a 25% tariff on European steel and aluminium, prompting the EU to prepare retaliatory levies on USD 28 billion of US goods, effective April, amplifying trade war concerns. The US dollar (USDOLLAR,DXY) weakened to a five-month low. Equity markets staged a robust recovery, with the STOXX Europe 600 advancing 8.3% year-to-date, outpacing the S&P 500. European equities, trading at a forward P/E of 14x versus 22x for US stocks, benefited from favourable valuations and ECB support.

China and emerging markets ('EM')

China’s economic trajectory in Q1 2025 was shaped by a delicate balance between policy recalibration, persistent disinflationary trends, and heightened geopolitical risks. While GDP surpassed 130 trillion yuan (USD 17.82 trillion) for the first time, growing at 5% in line with official targets, underlying momentum remained fragile. The National Bureau of Statistics reported contracting manufacturing activity and subdued consumer sentiment, with January PMIs disappointing and servicesector expansion losing steam. External headwinds intensified as escalating trade frictions with the US and seasonal distortions, including the Lunar New Year, contributed to an unexpected decline in imports and weakening export momentum. The People's Bank of China(PBoC) maintained a cautious monetary stance, prioritizing currency stability amid ongoing depreciation pressures, while inflation remained volatile, with a transient 0.5% rise in January followed by a sharp -0.7% contraction in February, reinforcing concerns over entrenched disinflation.

Chart 4: 1-year performance Hang Seng Tech vs Nasdaq 100

Equities rebounded on state intervention, led by a 24.7% YTD surge in the Hang Seng Tech Index, fuelled by President Xi’s corporate outreach and speculation over regulatory easing. Offshore equities remained vulnerable to geopolitical risks, while onshore markets displayed relative resilience. The PBoC’s suspension of bond purchases fuelled short-end volatility, flattening the yield curve. Geopolitical uncertainty loomed large, with the Trump administration’s tariff threats amplifying risks to China’s export-driven growth model. The yuan showed relative resilience, depreciating 0.5% YTD. Structural fragilities in the property sector, tepid private investment and constrained credit transmission mechanisms weighed on sentiment, reinforcing expectations that fiscal stimulus alone would prove insufficient to engineer a durable recovery.

Investment conclusions

The global economic landscape is undergoing a profound transformation, characterized by the dismantling of old structures and a realignment of power. Higher tariffs and increasing economic isolation are pushing up prices, resulting in long-term productivity losses and subdued growth prospects. Significant investments in defence and infrastructure provide a much-needed boost to stagnating regions like Europe, though this also creates delayed inflationary pressures. In response, markets are adjusting to higher risk premiums as rising debt levels compound the challenges. The US faces low growth, while the EU tries to break free from stagnation and China pursues its ambitious 5% growth target. Structural inflation, driven by de-globalization, tariffs and energy transition, intersects with geopolitical tensions and tighter financial conditions. Despite these challenges, large-cap technology stocks and the US housing market show resilience, while risk aversion permeates the broader market.

Chart 5: Yield-to-worst of global bond segments

Bonds: Fixed income offers compelling total return opportunities amid widening credit spreads and a steeper European yield curve. We favour short-duration high-yield and loans, while duration remains an effective portfolio diversifier.

Equities: Fiscal stimulus in Europe and China, alongside USD weakness, bolsters ex-US equities short-term. We maintain a positive equity bias with a blended style approach, acknowledging persistent volatility and limited upside in large-cap US equities.

The tactical approach emphasizes balance, with a slight preference for value and cyclicals amid resilient growth. Duration is neutral, maintaining a balanced view on IG bonds and USTs, while favouring short-term HY, loans and selective below-IG bonds and hybrids.

data.title: Domino's Pizza: A Slice Of Profitable Growth - Why Investors Should Take A Bite
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Abhishek Gajula as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

Domino's Pizza (NASDAQ:DPZ) has showcased robust growth through its unique technology driven business approach and widespread network of franchises, achieving tremendous market share in the Quick Service Restaurant sector. DPZ's forward price to earnings (P/E) Ratio of nearly 27x and enterprise value to EBITDA of about 22x suggests that the stock is fairly priced in coordination with projected future growth. DPZ is expected to grow at about 7% annually for the next 10 years. (which i will be expanding in the growth estimates section). I rate Dominos as a definite BUY.

Introduction

Domino's Pizza is almost unanimously agreed upon to be a market leader in the global Pizza industry. With a franchise network spanning more than 20,000 stores across more than 90 different countries, Domino's has made for itself one of the largest business networks globally. It's unique market approach focusing mainly on digitality has contributed to about 75% sales (through online medium) in the U.S. This strategic market approach is a key factor in DPZ's dominating historical performance. While Investors may be concerned about a slight deceleration in growth, such as the Q4 Global retail sales falling to 4.4% compared to the previous years 6%, inflationary challenges and increased labor expenses, DPZ's operational income still increased by 6.5% in Q4, highlighting their consistent performance despite short term hurdles. Despite temporary headwinds, DPZ's valuation still appears to remain strong, considering factors such as the attractive growth potential globally and their ability to perform consistently mainly through their unique digital approach to the market. With strategic growth based reinvestments and innovative delivery models, DPZ is a stock well positioned to lock in its stance as a market leader and generate long term shareholder value.

Growth Estimates

I expect a 7% overall annual growth rate for Domino's Pizza in the years to come, driven majorly by balanced franchise expansion coupled with Same-store sales (SSS) increase. This estimate assumes an overall store count growth of about 4% annually, paired with a SSS growth of about 3%. Historical performance supports this assumption: Domino's revenue rose from $2.42 billion in 2015 to $4.71 Billion in 2024, achieving a CAGR of 7.2%. In 2024 alone, Domino's added about 775 new stores across the world, totaling up to 21,366 stores worldwide, which is a tremendous 3.76% increase while global retail sales grew by about 4.64%, with U.S Same store sales standing at 3.2% and international same store sales at 1.6%. I’ve taken 3% same store sales growth rate as it is in-line with the Global economic growth rate. Also Dominos has enjoyed a 3.8% increase in the number of new stores over the past year, highlighting comfortable growth over recent years. Here is a look at DPZ’s store count growth over the years:

Historically Dominos has achieved a fascinating growth rate of about 5% for their store count across the globe (Refer Graph Above). However taking a conservative stance, I assume the store count growth to hover around 4% annually, inline with growth projections for global economy. This growth would help Dominos reach their overall store count to about 31,000 by 2032. Something interesting I've observed here: DPZ has opened more stores internationally over the last year than domestically, showcasing strong commitment and potential for global expansion. I've assumed a 4% overall growth rate for DPZ mainly driven by their aggressive store count expansion. Post 2032, Assuming that the store count stays constant (from a conservative viewpoint) , We can estimate the same store sales to grow by about 4% which falls inline with the global economic growth projections. Therefore I estimate a terminal growth rate of about 4%.

Is Domino's Pizza's Debt a risk?

Dominos has a debt of about $3.83 Billion (As of December 2024). This debt was majorly taken to further boost expansion and grow their store network. The Debt to EBITDA ratio stands at about 3.79x, the ratio being slightly on the higher end. This means the company needs about 3.7 years of consistent and stable performance to repay all it's debts.

Domino's Pizza ability to manage their Debt's efficiently is what makes it an attractive opportunity to me. The reported interest coverage ratio for the company stood at 4.5x (As of December 31, 2024). This particular ratio showcases the ability of Dominos to deliver stability on a consistent basis, managing their interest payments without compromising their shareholder returns. Adding on, the company's total debt has remained stable. These figures suggest that Domino's maintains a well-thought approach to debt management, ensuring its financial obligations are well-taken care of. This debt has so far managed to be positive to shareholders as it boosted the overall growth and expansion of the company.

DCF valuation

As discussed earlier I've assumed a 7% growth rate (during the projected period until 2032) followed by a 4% terminal growth rate for future growth of DPZ, highlighting the future expansion that I am assuming for Dominos over these 7 years. This growth is mainly driven by DPZ's rapid store count growth coupled with ever strengthening brand identity. I am assuming 9% discount rate as it is inline with earlier market growth rate.

The valuation model I've taken gives us a resulting share value of about $447, signaling that the stock is fairly valued. This indicates that the stock is fairly priced for a 9% return i.e The Dominos Shareholder can comfortably expect a 9% return annually, assuming that the company's performance is inline with projected growth rate. Temporary market fluctuations could impact DPZ's returns, yet the strong branding of Dominos coupled with rapid store count increase and consistent innovation in marketing strategies would eventually stabilize the situation putting dominos back on track in terms of stable returns. However here are a few risks I would like to discuss upon when it comes to a stock like Dominos.

Risks Involved for Potential Shareholders

Excess Dependence on store count growth

Domino's Pizza as of my observation depends heavily upon physical store count growth, mainly in international markets, for its overall expansion. I've assumed a 4% annual increase in store counts to support the overall 7% growth rate. However, the assumption I've taken comes with a few risks. Expanding Internationally through physical outlets involves complex tasks such as regulation problems, cultural & economic differences, economic instabilities which stand as an obstacle for healthy growth even for a company as DPZ. U.S being a mature market, limits growth opportunities, automatically making international expansion seem like a better option for most companies. Any failure to execute this expansion effectively, could take a solid hit on Domino's growth leading to a potential down which investors do not prefer.

Debt Burden

I've discussed earlier that DPZ carries a debt of $3.83 Billion (as of December 2024) with a debt to EBITDA ratio of about 3.79. Although the reported interest coverage ratio is 4.5, from which I would infer that DPZ can handle interest payments efficiently, There is always a possibility for short term market fluctuation, making it a minor risk for Investors. I'd say minor because for a company like domino's which relies heavily upon its well established market model, such temporary fluctuations leave behind a minimal effect, But the risk still persists.

Margin Pressure

Dominos recently has been facing increased pressure on their margins pertaining to operations, mainly because of labor cost increase. As I've mentioned earlier Although there were Higher sales in 2024, the Operating income in Q4 2024 grew by only a 6.5% signaling the fact that cost increases are more as compared to revenue gains. Factors such as Inflation of Labor, Raw materials and disruptions in supply chains could further reduce overall returns. This further reduces possibilities for reinvestments in growth initiatives, challenging DPZ to maintain its stock value.

Who is this a right fit for?

Growth Oriented Investors with Moderate Risk Tolerance

In-line with the growth estimate I've taken earlier. I Project DPZ to grow at an overall growth rate of 7% with a 4% terminal growth rate combined by 3% same store sales growth. With Forward P/E of 27x and EV/EBITDA of about 22x, DPZ to me is fairly valued. Domino's is a must buy to investors seeking growth at a comfortable and a reasonable price making it probably one of the best available options.

Long Term / Value based investors

A valuation of about $447 per share indirectly signals the 9% annual return rate I am assuming for DPZ, making it a fruitful bet for Investors who are willing to look at a 5-10 year game. DPZ's Expansionary strategies combined with a conservative stance (As discussed earlier), with projection to reach 31,000 stores worldwide by 2032 highlight DPZ's long term growth potential perfect for value based investors making DPZ a perfect fit for their Portfolio.

Defensive Investors

DPZ is a company that has a reliable market stance irrespective of temporary headwinds and market fluctuations. Defensive Investors who tend to value stable returns and resilient market stance will find Dominos as a fruitful and promising bet, mainly due to their projected overall growth rate which in my assumption is 7%. Coupled with my 9% annual return rate, DPZ is projected to deliver consistent performance while delivering the extra layer of protection Defensive investor's look for.

Conclusion

Dominos stands tall as a promising buy for all investors looking at a stock that ensures long term value based growth. Being a market leader in the Quick Service Restaurant Sector, Domino's is a brand that is backed by its fruitful franchise growth across continents and digitality based market approach. I project Domino's to possess a global network of about 31,000 stores by the year 2032. This growth assumption alone, even from a conservative stance, is a testament to DPZ's future growth potential ensuring consistent returns, Which makes it an absolutely attractive buy for me. Although there are temporary fluctuations and growth obstacles, DPZ is one such company that can manage these situations effectively. To me, Dominos is an opportunity that promises consistency coupled with stability. This isn't just any other stock, I consider it a slice of tomorrow's profits.

data.title: Flowers Foods: Simple Mills May Not Salvage Stagnating Sales
data.description:
Flowers Foods, Inc. (FLO) produces and sells bakery food products like bread and cakes in the United States. The company owns brands such as Nature’s Own, Dave’s Killer Bread, Canyon Bakehouse, Wonder, and the recently acquired Simple Mills among others. Flowers sells to retail channels and to foodservice customers.

Despite an overall solid earnings performance, Flowers’ stock has lost value in the past decade. The company is facing recent concern over the rise of private label brands in a cautious consumer environment, causing Flowers’ organic performance prospects to weaken. The company does pay out a good and growing dividend, still, with a current yield of 5.1%.

Growing Private Label Penetration Weakens Flowers’ Prospects

Over the long term, Flowers’ financial performance has been fair. Through acquisitions and modest organic growth, the company has grown revenues at a fair 2015-2024 CAGR of 3.4%. Profitability has also been fair and relatively stable with the operating margin varying from a low of 6.0% to a high of 8.1% during the period. Flowers’ return on capital stands at a 8.7% level, being fair for a company in the consumer staples sector. In 2024, Flowers’ revenues stood at $5.10 billion and operating income at $380 million at a 7.4% margin.

Against the long-term trend, Flowers’ more recent earnings performance hasn’t been as good. Revenues only grew 0.2% in 2024, and in Q4, revenues continued to increasingly slow down with a year-on-year decline of -1.6%. Flowers’ sales volume declined -2.5%, not being offset by pricing actions. The volume decline reflects Flowers’ weakening market share in the cake segment. In the more important bread segment, Flowers’ market share has been sustained better, albeit the segment’s market share also had a sequential hiccup in Q4.

Behind the decline, the consumer environment has been poor for Flowers. After severe food inflation in previous years, consumers have become more price-conscious in grocery shopping, causing private label brands to gain market share against branded products after 2021. As the consumer sentiment has remained weak, and consumer trends are continuing to shift into cheaper alternatives, Flowers’ demand outlook remains quite weak - the strong brand portfolio should continue to garner interest, but at a likely decreasing rate. With very slow projected growth for industry-wide bread sales in the US, the market share pressure seems likely to prolong Flowers’ recent issues in maintaining sales volumes.

Flowers has chosen to focus on maintaining and growing margins instead of revenue growth – going forward, the company is focusing extensively on its brand and product portfolio to prioritize high-margin sales. Against weak sales volumes in a competitive environment, Flowers still increased pricing by 0.9% in Q4 against deflation in ingredient costs and other cost savings, causing a 90 basis point expansion in the gross margin. Wheat futures have been moving favorably for Flowers after 2022, recently improving the company’s margin prospects slightly against weak sales trends. Ultimately, I still believe that Flowers’ margins are likely to be pressured by the weak sales prospects.

For 2025, Flowers guides around a 0.8% mid-point organic revenue growth when excluding the fiscal year’s 53rd week and the acquisition of Simple Mills, and adjusted EBITDA is expected to decline by around $4.5 million on a comparable basis to $534 million. Flowers still targets annual revenue growth of 1-2% and EBITDA growth of 4-6% over the long term, but in light of the 2025 guidance and recent industry & financial trends, I believe that the EBITDA growth ambition needs to be taken with fair skepticism.

Simple Mills Acquisition Could Aid Growth

Flowers recently acquired Simple Mills for $795 million in cash, expanding the company’s presence in better-for-you snacks. The acquisition was financed with debt, increasing Flowers’ net debt to approximately $1.9 billion at the time of the transaction's closing.

Simple Mills generated 2024 revenues of $240 million, making the acquisition come at quite an expensive 3.3 EV/S multiple. For comparison, Flowers only trades at a 1.1 EV/S, making the acquisition very expensive compared to Flowers’ own valuation. The acquisition does come with a better EBITDA margin profile, though.

The rationale for the Simple Mills acquisition seems more strategic than financial. Against weak organic sales trends for Flowers, Simple Mills’ revenues grew 14% in 2024 due to growing consumer interest in the better-for-you snacking category. The acquisition now slightly refuels Flowers’ poor organic growth prospects. Under Flowers, Simple Mills will also be able to expand distribution and product categories, bolstering the acquisition’s sales growth outlook further.

I believe that the acquisition is quite neutral for Flowers. The price tag is expensive, and the acquisition increases Flowers’ debt very notably. On the other hand, Flowers clearly needs to bolster growth, and the acquisition clearly provides a pathway for growth. Simple Mills’ scale is still less than 5% of Flowers’ total revenues, though, and the acquisition likely won’t completely turn around Flowers’ weak organic growth prospects.

Valuation

I built a discounted cash flow ((DCF)) model to estimate a fair value for Flowers’ stock.

While Flowers’ mid-term revenue growth will benefit from the Simple Mills acquisition, I ultimately estimate the company’s revenue growth to stabilize at a very low 1% annual level. The level suggests stagnating sales volume and modest long-term inflation in bread.

As I estimate Flowers’ sales volume to stagnate, and that private label competition will pressure the company, I don’t expect Flowers’ margin performance to improve as within the company’s target algorithm. Instead, in a base scenario, I estimate the EBIT margin to stabilize at 7%, still well within the company’s historical range. The 2025 organic guidance also suggests margin pressure.

I expect Flowers’ cash flow conversion to be quite good. I also added $795 million of debt to the model due to the Simple Mills acquisition.

The estimates put Flowers’s fair value at $19.85, 5% above the stock price at the time of writing. The valuation seems to already price in a performance well below Flowers’ own long-term targets, making the valuation fair for now in my opinion.

Flowers’ forward EV/EBITDA of 9.4 is below the stock’s long-term average, but due to Flowers’ increasingly pressured sales, I believe that the gap is justified.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 5.68%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 5.75% interest rate, being Flowers’ recent 2035 senior notes’ interest rate. I estimate a fairly high 35% long-term debt-to-equity ratio as the Simple Mills acquisition increases Flowers’ debt.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.18% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. Yahoo Finance estimates Flowers’s beta at 0.40. With a liquidity premium of 0.25%, the cost of equity stands at 6.16% and the WACC at 5.68%.

Takeaway

Flowers’ earnings prospects are increasingly pressured by the rise of private label brands, making the company’s organic sales outlook weak. The company is clearly focusing on protecting margins with strong brands, but I believe that the competitive pressure causes quite a weak earnings growth outlook. To revitalize growth prospects, Flowers recently acquired Simple Mills, but the acquisition was relatively expensive and doesn’t completely shift Flowers’ growth profile. The stock’s valuation already reflects weak growth expectations, though, and I initiate Flowers Foods at a Hold rating as a result.

data.title: Why Ideaya Biosciences Is Down Despite Many Positives
data.description:
Recently, I got a complaint from a frustrated reader who pointed out that since my bullish December coverage of Ideaya Biosciences, Inc. (NASDAQ:IDYA), the stock has gone down relentlessly and is now down ~50%. A number of factors come into play in taking IDYA down - some of these are macroeconomic or Trump related factors affecting the whole biopharma space, and some are IDYA specific. Lets take a look.

Trump’s decimation of the FDA

President Donald Trump’s policies have led to mass firings in the healthcare sector, with the “U.S. Department of Health and Human Services … implementing a massive “reduction in force,” with 8,000 to 10,000 workers losing their jobs as early as Friday,” says a report from Barron’s published last week. Another article published yesterday says the impact on the FDA “has been dire.” There have been 3500 job cuts at the FDA, with key figures like Dr Peter Marks of the Center for Drug Evaluation and Research's Office of New Drugs, and Dr Peter Stein of the Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research resigning within days of each other. These two are key departments at the FDA, and with upcoming regulatory approvals, any changes at either will have major impacts for IDYA.

According to RBC Capital Markets analyst Brian Abrahams, there have been “changes across the cell and gene therapy units as well as cancer drug reviewers,” both of which impact IDYA. Potential for price hikes and further scrutiny on such hikes are not going to help companies with upcoming approvals, like IDYA.

Trade Tariffs and Manufacturing Costs

President Trump will be announcing more tariffs today, and this is having an immediate, near term effect on the entire biopharma industry. GlobalData analyst Adam Bradbury says that this will impact “production costs and drug prices” across the board.

Take darovesertib, IDYA’s lead asset, as an example. Licensing it from Novartis, IDYA has established manufacturing supply chains in countries such as Hong Kong (China):

We have established collaborative relationships with other companies for access to their proprietary database of patient samples, and/or for their genetic screening services on their proprietary platform. We have established certain development manufacturing and service relationships with CMOs for darovasertib, IDE397, and IDE161, as well as our preclinical candidates IDE892 and IDE251. We have an agreement with STA Pharmaceutical Hong Kong Limited, or STA Pharmaceutical, and Yuhan Corporation for the synthesis of the API for darovasertib, and agreements with STA Pharmaceutical and Patheon Inc. for formulation and manufacturing of darovasertib drug product. We have an agreement with STA Pharmaceutical for the synthesis of the API, formulation and manufacturing of IDE397 drug product. We have an agreement with Pharmaron for the synthesis of the API, and with STA Pharmaceutical for the formulation and drug product manufacturing of IDE161, IDE892 and IDE251. We have established arrangements with CMOs as well for packaging, labeling and distribution of darovasertib, IDE397, and IDE161. We also have established clinical services relationship with CROs to support our conduct of clinical trials for our darovasertib, IDE397, and IDE161 programs.

Trump’s tariffs will have a huge impact on Chinese manufacturing of these APIs, and reciprocal tariffs from China are not going to help the situation.

Impact on US-China biotech collaborations

I have long held that US-China biotech collaborations reside in murky waters, where data bridging is at least the minimum requirement, with many US officials looking askance at many aspects of such collaborations. Now, with Trump’s America first policies, the US-China research collabs have been especially hit. A recent article in FiercePharma describes how:

On Feb. 21, Trump issued a memorandum titled the “America First Investment Policy” for several federal leaders. Emphasizing national and economic security, the policy aims to restrict both inbound and outbound investments related to “foreign adversaries” in certain strategic industries. The document puts China front and center and mentions both healthcare and biotechnology among the sectors it will regulate.

Of the three types of impactful changes described there, the most relevant for IDYA is the following on in-licensing Chinese drugs:

First, U.S. biopharma companies risk losing a large pool of potential drug candidates for in-licensing—if such transactions were to be considered a type of “investment” blocked under the Trump administration.

A number of analysts argue that in-licensing is not investing per se, so these policies may not impact in-licensing. However, I have grave doubts - in my opinion, Trump’s intent is not just to make a play on the meaning of the word “investment,” but it is a conservative mindset aimed at bringing not just investment but also innovation back to America.

How does that impact IDYA? Last year, IDYA entered into licensing agreements with a Chinese drugmaker for two key molecules:

Additionally, in 2024, we entered into two in-licensing agreements for ADCs with topoisomerase-I-inhibitor-payloads to enable combinations with our synthetic lethality programs. Pursuant to the Biocytogen Option and License Agreement, we obtained an option to in-license IDE034, a preclinical B7H3/PTK7 bispecific ADC, and we subsequently exercised the option to obtain worldwide commercial rights to the molecule. We also entered into the Hengrui Pharma License Agreement for global development and commercial rights to IDE849, a DLL3-targeting ADC, outside of Greater China. Under the terms of the Biocytogen Option and License Agreement and the Hengrui Pharma License Agreement, Biocytogen and Hengrui Pharma provide development manufacturing services for the ADCs.

While nothing is still certain under the new regime, the entire industry is reeling from the potential impacts of these policies, and IDYA seems no exception.

Market sentiment, short interest and analyst downgrades

Coming back to IDYA-specific issues, there’s a 11.29% short interest on the stock right now. Also, of the around 87 million shares outstanding, around 9 million, or ~11%, are sold short; both of these reflect negative market sentiment.

While many analysts remain bullish, Leerink Partners’ Andrew Berens issued negative reports on IDYA late last year. Lowering the price target from $41 to $27, they also issued a downgrade, citing “uncertainty in key programs darovasertib and IDE397.” For daro’s frontline HLA- metastatic uveal melanoma, Leerink sees a catalyst desert until next year. More interestingly, for IDYA’s lead program, here’s what the analyst says:

For the neoadjuvant program, the firm is confident in daro’s ability to demonstrate benefit in the smaller pool of enucleation directed patients. However, based on MEDACorp KOL feedback, Leerink sees significant risk in daro’s ability to translate to a benefit in vision loss in the larger pool of brachyradiotherapy patients.

Recall from my article that there was a 50% eye preservation rate for the enucleation neoadjuvant IST cohort, however, this did not immediately translate to a benefit in vision loss in brachyradiotherapy patients.

Closing the Amgen deal

In February, IDYA took the lead in closing a deal with Amgen (AMGN). The deal partnered IDYA’s MAT2A inhibitor IDE397 with Amgen’s MTA-cooperative PRMT5 inhibitor AMG 193. I have not seen many smaller biotech closing out of deals with big pharma like this, but IDYA seems to have its reason. The reason is that IDYA now has its own PRMT5 inhibitor, and does not need Amgen any more. That inhibitor is IDE892, which “it plans to test with IDE397 in people with MTAP-deletion non-small cell lung cancer in the second half of the year.”

Luckily, it seems IDE892 is both internally developed and wholly owned, which does not go against Trump’s America First policy. Manufacturing is another issue, however - the company outsources manufacturing of IDE892 to British and China-based companies, so that may go against the new policies of the US government.

Risks to my thesis

It is not exactly my thesis that IDYA has gone down for these reasons; it is just that I am considering all these as purported reasons. It all boils down to whether lead asset daro will be approved soon, and whether it will be able to penetrate its target market. Even so, the company is making certain right moves in the regulatory and commercialization direction which may help it break through.

One of these is hiring Stu Dorman from Gilead as chief commercial officer. At Gilead, Dorman helped launch Trodelvy, with which IDYA has recently collaborated for IDE397 in NSCLC. At BMS, where he worked for 14 years, Dorman handled the global commercialization of Yervoy and Opdivo.

In February, the company also hired Joshua Bleharski as CFO. Joshua previously worked as the managing director and global co-head of Biopharma of J.P. Morgan's Healthcare Investment Banking group. These are key hires of industry veterans in preparation for commercialization.

Many of IDYA’s positives I discussed in earlier articles. One key positive is that darovasertib is targeting HLA-A2 negative NSCLC patients, which sets it apart from Immunocore’s Kimmtrak, which only targets HLA-A2 positive patients, which constitute around 45% of the "Western" population. Thus, HLA-A2 -ve is the larger, untapped market.

Financials

IDYA has a market cap of $1.43bn and a cash balance of $1.1bn. Last quarter, it had revenues of $7mn through a GSK milestone payment. Also note:

Subsequent to the reporting period for the year ended December 31, 2024, the Company generated net proceeds of approximately $25.1 million from the sale of shares of its common stock through at-the-market (ATM) offerings in January 2025.

Research and development (R&D) expenses for the three months ended December 31, 2024, were $140.2 million, while G&A expenses were $11.0 million. Their CFO for the quarter was ($121.7mn), while capex was ($1.1mn), giving them an FCF of -$128.8mn and a cash runway of 10 quarters.

Bottom Line

IDYA is down ~50% since my article, and frankly, none of the IDYA-specific reasons seem too relevant. I believe the new Trump policies could be wreaking havoc on the company, but that they are doing for the entire sector - which is not sustainable and will be lobbied hard against, and changed. As such, for risk-savvy investors, and given its potential, IDYA is still a buy.

data.title: ON Semiconductor: A Buy With Caution Advised
data.description:
ON Semiconductor (NASDAQ:ON) revenue has declined 36% since its previous peak of the last semiconductor cycle. The stock has also declined more than 60% as a result. While investors need to be cautious of macroeconomic and geopolitical risks, the stock is a buy at these prices.

Background

Analog and Power semiconductor market has been declining since the last quarter of 2023. The chart below shows the revenue decline of key semiconductor players in analog and power from their previous quarterly revenue peak in the last cycle. For those who are not familiar with the background, COVID lockdowns coupled with the easing of monetary policy increased the demand for semiconductors which resulted in an unprecedented chip shortage. Chip customers were looking for every wafer they could get their hands on. But the wafer supply is not elastic. As the customers started to get desperate, they started to double or even triple book with the chip suppliers. It was a golden period for chip suppliers who were enjoying increasing margins and record profitability. During this period, chip suppliers started to push for long-term agreements with their customers. In order to avoid double bookings, they also started to make the orders NCNR (Non-cancelable, non-refundable) to which chip buyers obliged. This was the time when stocks of all major analog and power semiconductor players hit their all-time high. By the middle of 2023, ON Semiconductor (ON) stock was trading at $105.

Fast forward, the global economy opened up after COVID. Consumer behavior changed as the money started to flow back in tourism, restaurants and other outdoor activities. Semiconductor demand started to dry up and has been drying up since then except in the AI space. Today, ON Semiconductor (OnSemi) stock is trading at ~$40, a 60%+ decline since its peak in mid-2023. In this article, I will go through the state of OnSemi business to see if it makes sense for investors to buy OnSemi at these prices.

The Good

First thing to notice in OnSemi’s financials is that it has managed to maintain a decent net margin despite the decline of revenues this cycle. This is a result of their focus on high-growth, high-margin markets like automotive electrification, industrial automation and energy infrastructure.

Moreover, ON has changed its “Fab liter” strategy and adopted a “Fab right” strategy which includes selling fabs in Belgium and Japan focusing on less profitable segments such as consumer electronics. “Fab right” strategy focuses on expanding production capacity of high value power electronics products in New Hampshire and South Korea. ON has also upgraded its CMOS Image sensing business production. Transition of EastKill New York fab to 300mm allows better cost structure as compared to the competitors who are still producing chips with 200nm wafers.

Another positive in OnSemi’s solid M&A strategy. Just recently, they bought Qorvo’s Silicon Carbide (SiC) JFET business including its United Silicon Carbide (UnitedSiC) subsidiary—for just $115 million in cash. To add some context to the history of that business, Qorvo had previously acquired UnitedSiC, back in November 2021. According to SEC filings, Qorvo completed that acquisition for a total purchase price of approximately $234.2 million. Essentially, Qorvo spent around $234.2 million to bring UnitedSiC under its umbrella, later deciding to divest its SiC JFET business assets to Onsemi for just $115 million. OnSemi, is second in SiC market share, after STMicro. This acquisition essentially positions OnSemi for a number #1 position in SiC (risk factors included at the later sections of this article).

OnSemi's overall product portfolio is well diversified, reducing the overall risk if it loses competitiveness in one of its product lines. OnSemi is a strong incumbent in automotive image sensing (camera) solutions with a 40% market share. Within its intelligent sensing group, it also offer Lidar, Radar and Time of flight sensors with applications in autonomous vehicles and industrial robotics applications. Its Power Solutions Group (45% of the revenues) offers discrete, module, and integrated semiconductor products for power switching, signal conditioning, and circuit protection. Analog and Mixed Signal group (37% revenue) designs analog, mixed-signal, PMIC, sensor interface devices, and voltage regulation devices for various markets, especially focusing of automotive and industrial applications. With this breadth in its portfolio, combined with the long lifetime of revenue per project in automotive and industrial segments, the risk of rapid market disruption is quite low.

The Bad

As mentioned earlier, the bad news is the demand situation. In the last earning report, ON has forecasted its first-quarter 2025 revenue to be in the range of $1.35 billion to $1.45 billion, which is below analysts' consensus estimates of approximately $1.69 billion. During the call, CEO Hassane El-Khoury repeatedly mentioned that the market remains very uncertain and that there is no clear indication that the bottom has been reached yet.

Regarding the timing of the recovery, I went through the latest quarterly transcripts of all major OnSemi competitors. ADI and Infineon are claiming that Q4’2024 is the bottom and gradual recovery has already started. On the other hand, TI/NXP are more cautious in their outlook, hoping that the second half will start a long term recovery for the market. Microchip and ST, like OnSemi have cautioned that the bottom has not been in sight and the outlook is uncertain due to delays in eliminating excess inventory at customers (Microchip) and a sluggish European market (ST). While there are differences in the degree of recovery, there are no clear drivers that will lead to a rapid recovery for any of these companies in the second half of 2025, and the market environment is expected to continue to require caution.

On top of the already struggling Industrial and Automotive Segments, the uncertainty around tariffs and their impact of automotive sales is making the situation even worse. As supply side is inelastic in the short term, the proposed 25% tariffs on automotive will result in increased prices which will reduce the automotive demand further.

The Ugly:

The most concerning part is the supply demand situation is the outlook for Silicon Carbide business. On the demand side, Battery electric vehicle (BEV) shipment estimates have been revised downwards for 2024 and 2025 largely because of declining consumer interest, high vehicle prices, and decreased incentives for EV purchases in the European Union and the United States. China, the world’s largest EV market, is expected to see a somewhat more modest reduction in BEV production. The chart below shows the fading momentum in BEV shipments from McKinsey Center for Future Mobility, with a revised forecast of 20% EV penetration as compared to the base case of 27% share of EVs produced in 2027.

On the supply side, several semiconductor players have announced plans to build additional Silicon Carbide capacity. In 2023, global SiC install base was 2.8M wafers per year. If the suppliers keep up with their plans and don’t cancel some of the capex investments announced earlier, the SiC supply will be 10.9M wafers, 30% higher than the demand. That will result in a highly commoditized market. We believe that market participants are rational, especially in the “Rest of the World” segment. Some Chinese suppliers are motivated by market share instead of profitability and that might drive some non-Chinese players to either abandon their expansion plans or even entirely exit the market. This can potentially impact the outlook for OnSemi’s Silicon Carbide business which has been touted as a key growth driver for coming years.

Valuation:

To calculate the fair value of OnSemi stock, I have performed a reverse DCF analysis. For 2025-2028, I have taken the consensus earnings estimates from SeekingAlpha. Inline with the short term revenue risks, SeekingAlpha analyst consensus is that the FCF is expected to drop by ~37% this year followed by two high growth years in 2026 and 2027. Discount rate of 9.28% has been taken from AlphaSpread page which is inline with OnSemi peers: Microchip (MCHP) at 9.36%, STMicro (STM) at 9.41% and Analog Devices (ADI) at 9.35%.A perpetuity growth rate of 4% has been chosen as automotive semiconductor will outgrow global GDP growth due to adoption of electrification and autonomous vehicles. Plugging in these numbers into my reverse DCF model, we get a fair price of $58.67 which offers a 45.6% upside from its current price.

Conclusion:

In summary, while the current stock price looks attractive, I would like to reiterate that there are a couple of major risks involved. First, the bottom in automotive and industrial market is still not in sight. Second, there is a risk of commoditization of SiC business which is a key growth driver for OnSemi in coming years. I am not still going to buy OnSemi and will wait for some data points showing a growth in industrial and automotive segments again. For investors willing to take some risk, OnSemi is a buy but caution is recommended.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Tesla 'Fork In The Road Moment'
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Analyst Dan Ives says every metric is a ‘disaster.’ (0:15) Charles Schwab gets an upgrade. (3:15) ChatGPT sees soars in usage on ‘Ghibli effect.’ (3:42)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) fell short of expectations for Q1 deliveries as backlash against CEO Elon Musk and a changeover in the Model Y weighed. The EV maker reported 336,681 deliveries for Q1 shy of the 377,000 consensus and the lowest quarterly tally since 2022.

Tesla said it produced 362,615 vehicles during the quarter, and 4% of the deliveries total was subject to operating lease accounting.

Model 3/Y deliveries for the quarter were 323,800, while other models (the CyberTruck and Models S and X) accounted for 12,881 deliveries.

Wedbush analyst Dan Ives says: “We knew 1Q Tesla deliveries would be soft, but these numbers were bad. We are not going to look at these numbers with rose colored glasses … they were a disaster on every metric.”

“Refresh issues but brand crisis key. The time has come for Musk … fork in the road moment for Tesla.”

Tesla delivered 462,890 vehicles and 495,570 vehicles in the two preceding quarters and 386,810 vehicles in Q1 a year ago.

Looking ahead, analysts have not yet slashed their delivery forecasts for the remaining three quarters. The consensus estimates are for Q2 deliveries of 456,000, Q3 deliveries of 497,000, Q4 deliveries of 544,000. The optimistic forecasts are based on hopes that the new Model Y will be well received and that a sub-$30,000 model will finally be introduced. Also in the mix, full-scale production of the CyberCab could boost TSLA's deliveries numbers significantly in 2026 if demand for the two-seat robotaxi without a steering wheel takes off.

In addition, sales of Tesla's China-made electric vehicles fell 11.5% year-on-year in March amid intense competition. Tesla sold 78,828 China-made Model 3 and Model Y vehicles in the month, according to data from the China Passenger Car Association. But that was up sharply from the 30,688 units sold in February.

On the economic front, ahead of Friday’s jobs report, ADP said private sector employment climbed by 155,000 in March, topping the +120,000 consensus and almost double the 84,000 added in February.

But ADP’s track record has been dismal in predicting the official figures.

Among active stocks, BlackBerry (BB) was under pressure after the company issued an outlook for fiscal 2026 that fell short of Wall Street's estimates.

Looking to the next fiscal year, the BlackBerry said it expects revenue to be within a range of $504 million to $534 million, with the mid-point of $519 million, well below the $550.6 million analysts were expecting. It also expects adjusted EPS to be between $0.08 and $0.10, compared to the $0.10 estimate. For the upcoming quarter, it expects sales to be between $107 million and $115 million, below the $128.4 million analysts were anticipating.

And Citi upgraded Charles Schwab (SCHW) to Buy from Neutral as the firm pivots back to offense after completing its integration of Ameritrade.

Analyst Christopher Allen said: "We see potential to return to (a net new asset)/organic growth story, which, if executed while maintaining rate sensitivity/balance sheet discipline, should translate to multiple appreciation over time. Also, given SCHW’s capital generation capacity, we see an improving capital return story as well."

In other news of note, OpenAI's ChatGPT saw a record surge in usage last week after the rollout of the chatbot's viral "Ghibli effect" feature. That allows users to generate AI art inspired by Studio Ghibli's iconic hand-drawn animation style.

The animation imagery trend resulted in ChatGPT surpassing 150 million weekly active users for the first time this year.

"We added one million users in the last hour," OpenAI CEO Sam Altman said in a post. Upon ChatGPT's launch more than two years ago, 1 million users were reached in five days.

After the launch of its GPT-4o model that enables advanced image-generating capabilities, last week saw an all-time high in active users, in-app subscription revenue and app downloads, Reuters reported citing data from SensorTower.

The surge strained OpenAI's servers, leading to temporary outages and slower service. Altman acknowledged capacity challenges, saying delays in new releases and occasional breakdowns should be expected.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, BofA picked 9 high-conviction Buy-rated stocks and 1 Sell-rated stock across several industries for Q2.

Anthony Cassamassino, senior product marketing manager, says: “Uncertainty, around potential tariff announcements slated for April 2nd dominates market headlines. Our economics team expects a combination of reciprocal and sector-specific tariffs with a base case of a 5 percentage points increase in effective tariffs vs. pre-election, while our strategy team estimates a realistic bad case could result in a 10% hit to S&P EPS.”

Among the buys are Bath & Body Works (BBWI) with a price target of $45, as guidance “could prove conservative if collaborations drive incremental sales, candles return to growth or new launches gain incremental traction.”

And Goldman Sachs (GS) with a $700 price target. Analysts expect the “potential for a strong trading environment to persist given Trump policy actions and rapid changes to outlook for global monetary policy, regulatory relief on capital and on day-to-day running of the business (June stress test results watched), steady execution on improving firmwide profitability and growing the contribution from the asset and wealth management business all as potential catalysts to drive investor interest.”

The lone sell is Hims & Hers (HIMS) with a price target of $22.

“HIMS' recent revenue acceleration is driven by a loophole in federal drug regulations (FD&C Act and FDA guidance) that allows compound pharmacies to sell drugs while the branded versions are on shortage. However, this drug (semaglutide) is no longer in shortage, meaning HIMS will need to exit this business by May 22nd,” they said.

Check out all the picks.

data.title: Pinterest: A Differentiated Platform With A Massive Runway For Growth
data.description:
As of 3/5/25

Market Capitalization: $23.9B

Enterprise Value: $21.5B

Price: $35.20 (3/4)

What the Company Does

Pinterest (NYSE:PINS) is a social media platform that targets 553 million monthly active users (MAUs) with ads, as users browse content (aka “Pins”) to curate products and bring their ideas to life. The platform’s core use cases are women’s fashion, home décor, and food & drinks, with emerging use cases in men’s fashion, auto, and travel. Unlike other social media platforms, targeted ads on Pinterest represent highly relevant product ideas that blend in with the content the underlying user is actually seeking (e.g. new outfit). Therefore, Pinterest showing more ads to a user can actually lead to increasing engagement levels, creating a positive feedback loop that benefits users and advertisers. The Performance+ product allows digital advertisers to automate and optimize spending across their digital marketing campaigns on the platform.

Why We Own It

PINS is a differentiated social media platform with a massive runway for growth, as it looks to grow its <1% market share of the fast-growing >$600B global digital advertising market1 through a renewed strategic focus. Management’s decision to focus on more lucrative lower-funnel ads and enhancing “shopability” on the platform has delivered promising early results, as PINS more than doubled outbound clicks to retailers for four consecutive quarters before growing another 90% year-over-year (Y/Y) in 4Q24. Users are responding positively to the shift as well, as evidenced by 66% of weekly Gen Z users (>40% of Monthly Active Users (MAUs)) citing Pinterest as one of the first services they use for shopping (vs. 54% in prior year), along with MAUs and engagement levels reaching all-time highs in 4Q24. Third-party partnerships with Amazon (AMZN) and Google (GOOG,GOOGL) and the new Performance+ product are expected to further expand PINS’ addressable market by increasing international ad demand and lowering entry barriers for a wider range of advertisers. PINS is leveraging partnerships with digital ad resellers and Google Ads Manager to establish new advertiser relationships and improve monetization in international markets, which account for ~80% of PINS’ users but only ~25% of its revenue. Meanwhile, management cited a 20% reduction in cost per acquisition ('CPA') for advertisers testing their Performance+ product, which could result in increased allocations from smaller advertisers and further diversify PINS’ advertiser mix.

Management also plans to gain operating leverage through AI-based efficiencies, diminished sales and marketing costs from scaling 3rd-party partnerships, and mid-teens revenue growth, as PINS progresses towards its medium-term target for low-30% Adjusted EBITDA margins (vs. 28% in 2024). Despite tangible growth levers and robust profitability, shares trade at a forward (FY25) EV/EBITDA multiple of 16.8x, or a ~35% discount to peers (RDDT, SNAP, META, GOOGL, TTD).

How Management Allocates Capital

Management’s top capital allocation priority is investing in product and technology innovation, or R&D, to support its rapidly scaling network and improve the user experience. With a net cash position of $2.5B and no debt, management should have ample capacity to tap into its $1.9B share buyback program (8% of market cap) while meeting the business’s relatively low reinvestment needs, and preserving some optionality for opportunistic mergers and acquisitions (M&A).

1eMarketer Worldwide Digital Ad Spending Digital ad spend worldwide to pass $600 billion this year

EV/EBITDA Ratio compares a company’s enterprise value to its earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization and measures the value of a company.

data.title: Intuit Is A Fintech Fortress, But You're Paying For It Already
data.description:
Intuit Overview

Intuit (NASDAQ:INTU), best known for its tax return software, TurboTax, provides varying financial management services (e.g., Credit Karma, QuickBooks). The company is a highly successful Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) that generates billions each year in free cash flow (FCF), with FCF margins exceeding 30%. However, its stock in the past year has significantly underperformed industry benchmarks, returning -8% versus SPDR S&P 500 (SPY) gains of 6%.

Intuit's underperformance is despite consistent beats on revenue and EPS in 2024. So, what is happening?

The following article reveals that Intuit’s premium valuation, while justified, is making it difficult for the SaaS giant to outperform its required rate of return.

Earnings

On the income statement, Intuit divides its revenues into two categories: “product and other” and “services.” The former includes revenue from selling software licenses like Quickbooks. The latter regards income from ongoing services and subscriptions, including cloud-based offerings like TurboTax Live. In the three months ended January 31, 2025 (its fiscal Q2), service and product revenues were $3.249 billion and $714 million, respectively. The total represented 17% year-over-year revenue growth. Notably, its revenue mix leaned heavier on its service segment (82% versus 79.5% during the same period last year). Cost of service and product revenue was $880 million and $20 million, respectively. This makes sense. Licensing existing software involves very little cost. Providing services like “expert tax advice” is more costly. So, Intuit’s increasing reliance on services is probably impacting its overall profitability. This is unavoidable, though. Its software can only be licensed so many times, but services are perpetual. After accounting for S&M ($1.204 billion), R&D ($716 million), and G&A ($389 million) expenses, Intuit’s operating income in FQ2 was $593 million. Net income was $471 million. Looking at cash flows, Intuit’s FCF margin in FQ2 was 26%. In the trailing twelve months, its FCF margin was ~33%. Obviously, this can vary quarter to quarter, which is why I also present the annual figure.

Intuit reiterated its full fiscal year 2025 guidance. Revenue is expected to be between $18.16 billion and $18.347 billion, representing growth of 12%-13%.

Recent Developments

Intuit competes with many companies. However, anyone who's done taxes through a software like TurboTax knows how “sticky” it can be. No one wants to reference years of documents during tax season when a software keeps track of all that information for you. This makes it inconvenient to “switch.” This is great for Intuit because they are the market leader. So while the market becomes more saturated and competitive, Intuit has a good amount of “staying power.”

Last year, Intuit underwent restructuring to align with its AI-driven initiatives. This included laying off “1,800” employees, which represented 10% of its total workforce. Its intention was to hire employees with AI experience back. This resulted in a restructuring charge of $223 million in FQ4.

Intuit’s voyage into AI-assist solutions is best represented by its Intuit Assist, which utilizes generative AI to deliver personalized recommendations across products like TurboTax and QuickBooks. In addition, Intuit’s “agentic AI initiatives” streamline the onboarding process for new customers and automates processing of invoices. The careful and successful integration of AI into these products are so important for Intuit’s products moving forward. A lot of work in financial services is quite tedious and Intuit’s AI features could really help users become more efficient. Intuit’s robust cash generation and leadership in the financial services market really give it an edge here. It has the resources available to accelerate the development of these important features and remain differentiated from its competitors.

Financial Health

As of January 31, Intuit had $2.435 billion in cash and cash equivalents. Total current assets were $9.135 billion, while total current liabilities were $7.179 billion. Subsequently, Intuit’s current ratio is over 1, which signals solid liquidity. Intuit does owe $5.76 billion in long-term debt. Given Intuit’s robust cash generation, its debt appears manageable.

Valuation Assumptions

There are some caveats to consider when modeling Intuit in a discounted cash flow (DCF) model. Its fiscal period differs from calendar periods. So, my Year 1 input is an estimate for Intuit’s fiscal period ending July 2025. To account for this, I adjusted “Fraction of Year Elapsed” to 0.5 so that I do not double count cash flows (Intuit’s cash & equivalents are as of January 31). 14% year-over-year revenue growth and 32% FCF margins are a blend of historical performance and future expectations. Everything else is pretty standard.

So, on a pure cash flow basis, my model suggests Intuit is 9% overvalued.

My sensitivity analysis, which tells us how adjustments to key inputs (revenue growth and FCF margin) affect valuation, suggests that Intuit is priced for ~15.5% revenue growth or ~35% FCF margins.

Next, I ran a Monte Carlo simulation based on the inputs from my DCF model. The issue with my DCF model is that it presents only one scenario based on specific parameters. My Monte Carlo simulation accounts for variability in inputs like growth, margins, and beta

Intuit’s price still falls above the median of $556.61 per share.

Outside of "intrinsic value," another problem with Intuit is that its multiples also suggest the stock is valued at a premium. In the past few years, Intuit’s P/E ratio was as high as 90+. It has gradually compressed over time, but it is still high relative to its sector.

Admittedly, stocks are more complicated than their intrinsic value based on projected cash flows. They are often also priced by the market relative to peers and according to multiples like P/E and EV/EBITDA.

Over time, Intuit’s multiples are expected to contract more. This makes it challenging for the stock to outperform its required rate of return (9.88%) even when the company is delivering strong 14% revenue growth and 32% FCF margins.

INTU Stock: Investment Recommendation

In conclusion, Intuit’s established leadership, strong brand, and proactive AI investments should keep it on top of the competitive financial technology market. However, its stock appears slightly overvalued on a cash flow basis, and gradual multiple compression is making it difficult for INTU to outperform. This may continue for some time. However, Intuit could offer compelling upside in the reasonable event AI integrations help boost its profitability or growth outlook. This merits a “Hold” (market perform) recommendation. If Intuit can demonstrate operational improvements to boost cash generation or if its stock falls near Monte Carlo’s median value of $556.61, it should warrant a “Buy.” Until then, it’s challenging to envision INTU outperforming the market. Some risks to investment include continued stock underperformance despite fundamental strength, competition from other financial technology companies, regulatory changes, and macroeconomic headwinds.

data.title: Voya Intermediate Bond Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

Total return approach, investing across full spectrum of the fixed income market including up to 20% in below investment grade securities.

Portfolio review

Strong economic data continued to come through in the final quarter of 2024, which had the impact of driving spreads tighter while reversing the rate rally experienced in 3Q24. The gross domestic product report for 3Q24 once again came in elevated (initially reporting at 2.8% and later revised to 3.1%) driven largely by strong consumer and government spending, which grew at 3.7% and 5.1%, respectively. Notably, consumer spending accounted for roughly 80% of the overall growth, highlighting its critical role in the economy.

Inflationary pressures remained a key concern. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) surprised to the upside in early October, and while subsequent data showed stability in both CPI and Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) inflation, both remained elevated. Core PCE, the US Federal Reserve's preferred measure of inflation, finished the quarter at 2.8% year-over-year, slightly above where it was trending in the summer. Notably, core goods prices, which had experienced a deflationary trend for several months, began to reaccelerate.

Labor market dynamics continued to show signs of softening without entering a state of deterioration. Job gains remained reasonable, and although the unemployment rate resumed its upward trajectory, it remained below the high watermark set earlier in the year. Meanwhile, wage growth remained strong, which helps explains both the resilience of consumer spending and the stickiness of services inflation.

While the Fed continued to deliver rate cuts at both the November and December meetings, the December cut was accompanied by more "hawkish" elements. Specifically, the Fed's Dot plot indicated only two cuts projected for 2025 down from four in the previous iteration. Additionally, officials moved their projection for both growth and inflation higher, while their projection for the unemployment rate moved lower and only slightly above the current level.

Overall, the fourth quarter of 2024 was characterized by resilient labor market dynamics, strong economic growth and sticky inflation. The impact on fixed income performance was mixed, with yields rising and credit spreads tightening, leading to modestly negative total returns for most fixed income sectors.

For the quarter, the Fund outperformed the Index on NAV basis. Sector allocation and security selection decisions contributed, while duration and yield curve positions had minimal impact. Our overweight to credit and underweight in Treasuries broadly contributed. Our overweight to asset-backed securities (ABS) was the largest individual contributor, as the sector continued to tighten in sympathy with other high quality spread sectors. Our overweight to commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) was a close second, as fundamental factors improved, and the sector continued to benefit from an active primary market. Additionally, positive security selection results in CMBS were realized due to our bias towards higher yielding tranches. Security selection decisions with agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) also contributed, due to our off-benchmark collateralized mortgage obligation (CMO) holdings which outperformed despite underperformance of Index constituents. Finally, due to our neutral duration position, the move higher in rates had limited impact on relative performance.

Current strategy and outlook

Looking forward, we continue to expect economic growth will remain resilient. The recent election outcome further strengthens this view due to election optimism and productivity gains from deregulation. Meanwhile, we expect inflation to resume its downward trend, driven by moderating service prices. In this area however, the election result lowers our conviction as an adverse policy mix of deficit spending, tariffs and stricter immigration limits would sustain higher levels of demand while constraining supply. That said, we believe a negative market reaction would likely compel the administration to temper those policies before they derail growth.

Assuming this is the case, and inflation does not reaccelerate, we expect the Fed will tolerate inflation that is slightly above their target and deliver a few more rate cuts in an effort to preserve the current cycle.

Overall, this backdrop should be favorable for credit sectors, however we continue to favor high-quality, shorter-dated assets given where valuations are. Over the quarter we reduced our allocation to longer dated investment grade ((IG)) corporates and are now positioned with an underweight from a spread duration perspective. Similarly, we maintain only a modest allocation to high yield corporates, while high quality securitized makes up much of our active risk. If there is a misstep in policy implementation, we believe we are well positioned to capitalize on the short-lived market dislocation and will look to add market risk further out the curve.
